1:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying," The witnesses note the command now given for the lighting of the lamps, the candlestick already made. The witnesses note that Gill notes Aben Ezra thought God spoke this by night, when the lamps were lighted and burning. The witnesses note that Henry observes the candlestick was made long before (Exod. 25:31), but now first the lamps were ordered to be lighted, as the other holy things began to be used. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a new word — the LORD speaking again to Moses; the the candlestick ready — made long before, now first used; the the lamps lighted — the worship set fully on foot; the no darkness — the place of God's dwelling to be lit.

The witnesses agree the lighting of the lamps marked the sanctuary coming into full use: the candlestick had stood ready, but now its lamps were kindled, the worship of the holy place set fully on foot; the light in God's house was no afterthought but a needful part of his ordered service, the place of his dwelling not to be left in darkness.

So the witnesses make the verse the command for the lamps: The witnesses note the a new word: the LORD speaking again to Moses. The witnesses note the the candlestick ready: made long before, now first used. The witnesses note the the lamps lighted: the worship set fully on foot. The witnesses note the no darkness: the place of God's dwelling to be lit. The expositors observe the a new word: the LORD speaking again to Moses. The expositors observe the the candlestick ready: made long before, now first used. The expositors observe the the lamps lighted: the worship set fully on foot. The expositors observe the no darkness: the place of God's dwelling to be lit. The believer is taught to mark the a new word, the the candlestick ready, the the lamps lighted, the no darkness, and to be cleansed and given to God. The verse thus records that God now ordered the lamps lighted — the sanctuary coming into full use, the place of his dwelling not to be left in darkness.
2:
"Speak unto Aaron, and say unto him, When thou lightest the lamps, the seven lamps shall give light over against the candlestick." The witnesses note Aaron charged to light the seven lamps, that they give light before the candlestick. The witnesses note that Gill notes it was Aaron's work to light the lamps, though it belonged also to his sons, the common priests; this first time likely done by Aaron himself. The witnesses note that Gill sees Aaron here an eminent type of Christ, the great High Priest, who lights the lamps in all his golden candlesticks, the churches (Rev. 1:20). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Aaron lights them — his work, with his sons; the the seven lamps — giving light before the candlestick; the a type of Christ — the High Priest lighting his churches; the the golden candlesticks — the churches lit by their Lord (Rev. 1:20).

The reformed reader (Gill) draws the gospel figure: as Aaron kindled the lamps of the sanctuary, so Christ, the great High Priest, kindles and maintains the light in his churches, which are the golden candlesticks; from him they have all their light and all their oil, the light of the church being not its own but lit and fed by its Lord.

So the witnesses make the verse the seven lamps lit: The witnesses note the Aaron lights them: his work, with his sons. The witnesses note the the seven lamps: giving light before the candlestick. The witnesses note the a type of Christ: the High Priest lighting his churches. The witnesses note the the golden candlesticks: the churches lit by their Lord (Rev. 1:20). The expositors observe the Aaron lights them: his work, with his sons. The expositors observe the the seven lamps: giving light before the candlestick. The expositors observe the a type of Christ: the High Priest lighting his churches. The expositors observe the the golden candlesticks: the churches lit by their Lord (Rev. 1:20). The believer is taught to mark the Aaron lights them, the the seven lamps, the a type of Christ, the the golden candlesticks, and to be cleansed and given to God. The verse thus records that Aaron lit the seven lamps — a type of Christ the High Priest, who kindles and feeds the light in his churches, the golden candlesticks.
3:
"And Aaron did so; he lighted the lamps thereof over against the candlestick, as the LORD commanded Moses." The witnesses note Aaron's obedience — he lighted the lamps as the LORD commanded. The witnesses note that Gill notes that as God had taken up his residence in the most holy place and sacrifices were begun in the court, it was proper the service of the holy place be set on foot, the shewbread set and incense burned, which needed the lamps lit. The witnesses note that the lighting was done exactly as commanded. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the he lighted the lamps — exactly as commanded; the the holy place served — joined to the altar in the court; the the shewbread and incense — the inner service set on foot; the as the LORD commanded — the order of the sanctuary complete.

The witnesses agree the lighting completed the order of the sanctuary's service: with God dwelling in the holiest, the altar smoking in the court, and now the lamps burning in the holy place, the whole house was set in full operation; Aaron's simple obedience — "he did so" — joined the inner service to the outer, the light of the holy place shining as God had appointed.

So the witnesses make the verse Aaron's obedience: The witnesses note the he lighted the lamps: exactly as commanded. The witnesses note the the holy place served: joined to the altar in the court. The witnesses note the the shewbread and incense: the inner service set on foot. The witnesses note the as the LORD commanded: the order of the sanctuary complete. The expositors observe the he lighted the lamps: exactly as commanded. The expositors observe the the holy place served: joined to the altar in the court. The expositors observe the the shewbread and incense: the inner service set on foot. The expositors observe the as the LORD commanded: the order of the sanctuary complete. The believer is taught to mark the he lighted the lamps, the the holy place served, the the shewbread and incense, the as the LORD commanded, and to be cleansed and given to God. The verse thus records that Aaron lit the lamps in obedience — the inner service joined to the outer, the whole house of God set in full operation as he commanded.
4:
"And this work of the candlestick was of beaten gold, unto the shaft thereof, unto the flowers thereof, was beaten work: according unto the pattern which the LORD had shewed Moses, so he made the candlestick." The witnesses note the candlestick of beaten gold, made exactly according to the pattern shown to Moses. The witnesses note that Gill notes the candlestick was of beaten gold, made by the pattern shown in the mount. The witnesses note that Clarke reads the candlestick as an emblem of the church of Christ, the oil of the graces and gifts of the Spirit, and the light of those gifts in action among men (Rev. 1:12-20). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the beaten gold — wrought, shaped by the hammer; the the pattern in the mount — made exactly as God showed Moses; the the church — the candlestick an emblem of it; the the oil the Spirit — the graces and gifts giving light (Rev. 1:12-20).

The Wesleyan reader (Clarke) opens the figure: the golden candlestick is the church, the oil the graces and gifts of the Spirit, and the shining light those gifts and graces at work among men; God builds his church and sends his Spirit to dwell in and sanctify it, that it may give light — the beaten gold, wrought by blows, hinting that the church too is shaped by affliction into a vessel of light.

So the witnesses make the verse the golden candlestick: The witnesses note the beaten gold: wrought, shaped by the hammer. The witnesses note the the pattern in the mount: made exactly as God showed Moses. The witnesses note the the church: the candlestick an emblem of it. The witnesses note the the oil the Spirit: the graces and gifts giving light (Rev. 1:12-20). The expositors observe the beaten gold: wrought, shaped by the hammer. The expositors observe the the pattern in the mount: made exactly as God showed Moses. The expositors observe the the church: the candlestick an emblem of it. The expositors observe the the oil the Spirit: the graces and gifts giving light (Rev. 1:12-20). The believer is taught to mark the beaten gold, the the pattern in the mount, the the church, the the oil the Spirit, and to be cleansed and given to God. The verse thus records that the candlestick of beaten gold was made by God's pattern — an emblem of the church, the oil the Spirit's graces, the light those gifts at work among men.
5:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying," The witnesses note the command now turning to the consecration of the Levites to their service. The witnesses note that Gill notes this was given at the same time as the order about the lamps, the Levites already taken and numbered, now to be ordained and consecrated to their office. The witnesses note that Aben Ezra joins the sections to show Aaron lit the lamps himself but the Levites helped him in the rest of the service. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a new word — the LORD speaking again to Moses; the the Levites taken — already numbered, now to be ordained; the cleansed and dedicated — consecrated to their office; the servants set in order — the workmen of the sanctuary prepared.

The witnesses agree the chapter moves from the furniture of the sanctuary to its ministers: having ordered the lamps, God now orders the cleansing and dedication of the Levites who would serve under the priests; the holy place and its servants were both being set in order together, the worship of God needing not only its vessels and its light but its consecrated workmen.

So the witnesses make the verse the Levites' consecration: The witnesses note the a new word: the LORD speaking again to Moses. The witnesses note the the Levites taken: already numbered, now to be ordained. The witnesses note the cleansed and dedicated: consecrated to their office. The witnesses note the servants set in order: the workmen of the sanctuary prepared. The expositors observe the a new word: the LORD speaking again to Moses. The expositors observe the the Levites taken: already numbered, now to be ordained. The expositors observe the cleansed and dedicated: consecrated to their office. The expositors observe the servants set in order: the workmen of the sanctuary prepared. The believer is taught to mark the a new word, the the Levites taken, the cleansed and dedicated, the servants set in order, and to be cleansed and given to God. The verse thus records that God now ordered the consecration of the Levites — the worship of his house needing not only its vessels and light but its consecrated servants.
6:
"Take the Levites from among the children of Israel, and cleanse them." The witnesses note the Levites to be taken from among Israel and cleansed for their service. The witnesses note that Gill notes that henceforward they were distinguished from the Israelites, separated and appointed to their work as ministers to the priests. The witnesses note that, as Aaron took not the priesthood to himself but was called of God, so the Levites did not assume their office but were appointed to it. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the take the Levites — separated from among Israel; the a divine calling — not self-chosen but appointed by God; the cleanse them — purified for the holy service; the calling and cleansing — the pattern of true ministry.

The witnesses agree the Levites' ministry rested on a divine calling and required a cleansing: they were not self-chosen but taken out by God from among their brethren, and not admitted to the holy service until cleansed; the two together — calling and cleansing — mark the pattern of all true ministry, that none serve God in holy things who is not both called by him and purified for the work.

So the witnesses make the verse taken and cleansed: The witnesses note the take the Levites: separated from among Israel. The witnesses note the a divine calling: not self-chosen but appointed by God. The witnesses note the cleanse them: purified for the holy service. The witnesses note the calling and cleansing: the pattern of true ministry. The expositors observe the take the Levites: separated from among Israel. The expositors observe the a divine calling: not self-chosen but appointed by God. The expositors observe the cleanse them: purified for the holy service. The expositors observe the calling and cleansing: the pattern of true ministry. The believer is taught to mark the take the Levites, the a divine calling, the cleanse them, the calling and cleansing, and to be cleansed and given to God. The verse thus records that the Levites were taken from among Israel and cleansed — called by God and purified for the work, the pattern of all true ministry.
7:
"And thus shalt thou do unto them, to cleanse them: Sprinkle water of purifying upon them, and let them shave all their flesh, and let them wash their clothes, and so make themselves clean." The witnesses note the manner of the Levites' cleansing — sprinkling with water of purifying, shaving the flesh, and washing the clothes. The witnesses note that Gill notes the cleansing was Aaron's work, a type of Christ, the refiner and purifier of the sons of Levi (Mal. 3:3). The witnesses note that Clarke reads the "water of purifying" as the water of sin, made with the ashes of the red heifer, cedar, hyssop, and scarlet, the heifer's blood sprinkled. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the water of purifying — sprinkled, the water of the sin offering; the shave all the flesh — the hair wholly removed; the wash their clothes — a cleansing outward and complete; the Christ the refiner — purifying the sons of Levi (Mal. 3:3).

The witnesses agree the threefold rite pictured a thorough cleansing: the sprinkled water, the shaving away of all hair, and the washing of the clothes together signified a purifying outward and complete, fitting the Levites for holy service; Gill sees Christ in Aaron's work, the refiner of the sons of Levi, who purifies his ministers that they may offer to the LORD an offering in righteousness.

So the witnesses make the verse the threefold cleansing: The witnesses note the water of purifying: sprinkled, the water of the sin offering. The witnesses note the shave all the flesh: the hair wholly removed. The witnesses note the wash their clothes: a cleansing outward and complete. The witnesses note the Christ the refiner: purifying the sons of Levi (Mal. 3:3). The expositors observe the water of purifying: sprinkled, the water of the sin offering. The expositors observe the shave all the flesh: the hair wholly removed. The expositors observe the wash their clothes: a cleansing outward and complete. The expositors observe the Christ the refiner: purifying the sons of Levi (Mal. 3:3). The believer is taught to mark the water of purifying, the shave all the flesh, the wash their clothes, the Christ the refiner, and to be cleansed and given to God. The verse thus records that the Levites were cleansed by sprinkling, shaving, and washing — a thorough purifying, Christ the refiner of the sons of Levi fitting his ministers for holy service.
8:
"Then let them take a young bullock with his meat offering, even fine flour mingled with oil, and another young bullock shalt thou take for a sin offering." The witnesses note the two bullocks for the Levites' consecration — one for a burnt offering with its meal offering, one for a sin offering. The witnesses note that Gill notes the bullock was of two years old, as the Jewish writers gather. The witnesses note that Wesley observes the sin offering was the same offered for the whole congregation, because the Levites came in the stead of all the firstborn, who in a manner represented the whole congregation. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a burnt offering — the bullock with its meal offering; the a sin offering — another bullock to atone; the the congregation's offering — the Levites standing for all the firstborn; the washing and blood — both cleansing and atonement required.

The witnesses agree the sacrifices fitted the Levites' representative place: as they were taken instead of all Israel's firstborn, their sin offering was that of the whole congregation, atoning for them as standing for the nation; the cleansing by water was not enough — blood too was required, the Levites needing both washing and atonement before they could serve, as no minister draws near to God but through sacrifice.

So the witnesses make the verse the two bullocks: The witnesses note the a burnt offering: the bullock with its meal offering. The witnesses note the a sin offering: another bullock to atone. The witnesses note the the congregation's offering: the Levites standing for all the firstborn. The witnesses note the washing and blood: both cleansing and atonement required. The expositors observe the a burnt offering: the bullock with its meal offering. The expositors observe the a sin offering: another bullock to atone. The expositors observe the the congregation's offering: the Levites standing for all the firstborn. The expositors observe the washing and blood: both cleansing and atonement required. The believer is taught to mark the a burnt offering, the a sin offering, the the congregation's offering, the washing and blood, and to be cleansed and given to God. The verse thus records that two bullocks were taken for the Levites' consecration — both washing and atonement required, their sin offering that of the whole congregation they represented.
9:
"And thou shalt bring the Levites before the tabernacle of the congregation: and thou shalt gather the whole assembly of the children of Israel together:" The witnesses note the Levites brought before the tabernacle, the whole assembly of Israel gathered to witness. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were brought within, into the court of the tabernacle, where the altar of burnt offering stood. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch note Moses was to gather the whole assembly together for the act. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the before the tabernacle — the Levites set in the court; the the whole assembly — all Israel gathered to witness; the on their behalf — the dedication owned by the people; the in the sight of all — the ministry given to God publicly.

The witnesses agree the consecration was a public and congregational act: the Levites were set before the tabernacle and the whole nation gathered, that all might see their dedication and own it as done on their behalf; the ministry was given to God before the eyes of the people, the servants of the sanctuary consecrated not in secret but in the sight of all Israel, whose representatives they were.

So the witnesses make the verse the public dedication: The witnesses note the before the tabernacle: the Levites set in the court. The witnesses note the the whole assembly: all Israel gathered to witness. The witnesses note the on their behalf: the dedication owned by the people. The witnesses note the in the sight of all: the ministry given to God publicly. The expositors observe the before the tabernacle: the Levites set in the court. The expositors observe the the whole assembly: all Israel gathered to witness. The expositors observe the on their behalf: the dedication owned by the people. The expositors observe the in the sight of all: the ministry given to God publicly. The believer is taught to mark the before the tabernacle, the the whole assembly, the on their behalf, the in the sight of all, and to be cleansed and given to God. The verse thus records that the Levites were brought before the tabernacle and all Israel gathered — the ministry consecrated publicly, in the sight of the people whose representatives they were.
10:
"And thou shalt bring the Levites before the LORD: and the children of Israel shall put their hands upon the Levites:" The witnesses note the people laying their hands on the Levites, presenting them to the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were brought before the LORD, to the altar where he was present to accept the sacrifices. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read the children of Israel — the tribe-princes in the name of all — laying their hands on the Levites. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the before the LORD — the Levites presented at the altar; the the children of Israel — the princes in the name of all; the put their hands — offering the Levites as their gift; the a living offering — dedicated to God in place of the firstborn.

The witnesses agree the laying-on of hands was the people's act of giving: by it Israel offered the Levites to God as their own gift, in place of their firstborn, just as a worshipper laid his hand on the sacrifice he presented; the gesture transferred and dedicated, the whole nation through its princes putting the Levites into God's service as a living offering on their behalf.

So the witnesses make the verse the laying-on of hands: The witnesses note the before the LORD: the Levites presented at the altar. The witnesses note the the children of Israel: the princes in the name of all. The witnesses note the put their hands: offering the Levites as their gift. The witnesses note the a living offering: dedicated to God in place of the firstborn. The expositors observe the before the LORD: the Levites presented at the altar. The expositors observe the the children of Israel: the princes in the name of all. The expositors observe the put their hands: offering the Levites as their gift. The expositors observe the a living offering: dedicated to God in place of the firstborn. The believer is taught to mark the before the LORD, the the children of Israel, the put their hands, the a living offering, and to be cleansed and given to God. The verse thus records that Israel laid their hands on the Levites, offering them to God as their gift in place of the firstborn — the whole nation dedicating them as a living offering.
11:
"And Aaron shall offer the Levites before the LORD for an offering of the children of Israel, that they may execute the service of the LORD." The witnesses note Aaron offering the Levites as a wave offering before the LORD, that they might do his service. The witnesses note that Gill reads "offer" as "wave," signifying that the Levites belonged to the Lord of the whole earth, their readiness to serve, and their constant employment in his service, always in motion. The witnesses note that they were thus presented that they might execute the service of the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Aaron offers them — presenting the Levites before the LORD; the as a wave offering — moved in token of belonging to God; the the Lord's own — dedicated to him of the whole earth; the ever in service — a ministry not idle but always active.

The witnesses agree the waving of the Levites set them apart as wholly the LORD's and wholly given to his work: as the wave offering was moved before God in token of belonging to him, so the Levites were dedicated as a people in perpetual motion in his service; the figure speaks of a ministry that is not idle but ever active, the servants of God waved before him as those whose whole life is given to going about his business.

So the witnesses make the verse the Levites waved: The witnesses note the Aaron offers them: presenting the Levites before the LORD. The witnesses note the as a wave offering: moved in token of belonging to God. The witnesses note the the Lord's own: dedicated to him of the whole earth. The witnesses note the ever in service: a ministry not idle but always active. The expositors observe the Aaron offers them: presenting the Levites before the LORD. The expositors observe the as a wave offering: moved in token of belonging to God. The expositors observe the the Lord's own: dedicated to him of the whole earth. The expositors observe the ever in service: a ministry not idle but always active. The believer is taught to mark the Aaron offers them, the as a wave offering, the the Lord's own, the ever in service, and to be cleansed and given to God. The verse thus records that Aaron offered the Levites as a wave offering — set apart as wholly the LORD's, a ministry ever in motion in his service.
12:
"And the Levites shall lay their hands upon the heads of the bullocks: and thou shalt offer the one for a sin offering, and the other for a burnt offering, unto the LORD, to make an atonement for the Levites." The witnesses note the Levites laying their hands on the bullocks, the offerings making atonement for them. The witnesses note that Gill reads the laying-on of hands as transferring their sins to the bullocks, a figure of the imputation of sin to Christ, leading the offerers' faith to the sin-bearing Saviour. The witnesses note that the two bullocks atoned for the Levites, the one a sin offering, the other a burnt offering. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the lay their hands — the Levites putting their sins on the bullocks; the the imputation of sin — a figure of sin laid on Christ; the sin and burnt offering — the two victims atoning for them; the cleansed to serve — none fit for God's work but the atoned.

The reformed reader (Gill) finds here the doctrine of imputation in figure: as the Levites laid their hands on the bullocks and so put their sins upon them to be atoned, the rite taught and pointed to the imputing of sin to Christ, who bore the sins of his people; the very servants of the sanctuary needed atonement before they served, none being fit for God's work until his sin is laid on a substitute and put away.

So the witnesses make the verse atonement for the Levites: The witnesses note the lay their hands: the Levites putting their sins on the bullocks. The witnesses note the the imputation of sin: a figure of sin laid on Christ. The witnesses note the sin and burnt offering: the two victims atoning for them. The witnesses note the cleansed to serve: none fit for God's work but the atoned. The expositors observe the lay their hands: the Levites putting their sins on the bullocks. The expositors observe the the imputation of sin: a figure of sin laid on Christ. The expositors observe the sin and burnt offering: the two victims atoning for them. The expositors observe the cleansed to serve: none fit for God's work but the atoned. The believer is taught to mark the lay their hands, the the imputation of sin, the sin and burnt offering, the cleansed to serve, and to be cleansed and given to God. The verse thus records that the Levites laid their hands on the bullocks and were atoned for — a figure of sin imputed to Christ, the servants of God cleansed by atonement before they served.
13:
"And thou shalt set the Levites before Aaron, and before his sons, and offer them for an offering unto the LORD." The witnesses note the Levites set before Aaron and his sons, presented as an offering to the LORD. The witnesses note that Gill notes that after atonement was made, the Levites were presented to Aaron and his sons, to be their ministers and assistants. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch read them set before the priests and offered as an offering unto the LORD. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the after atonement — the Levites first cleansed and atoned; the before Aaron and his sons — presented as their ministers; the an offering to the LORD — given wholly to his work; the reconciled, then employed — the order of all true service.

The witnesses agree the order is significant: atonement first, then service; only after the Levites were cleansed and atoned were they set before the priests and given to the LORD's work; the pattern holds for all ministry — first reconciled, then employed, the servant fitted for the holy task by being first made clean and given wholly to God.

So the witnesses make the verse set before the priests: The witnesses note the after atonement: the Levites first cleansed and atoned. The witnesses note the before Aaron and his sons: presented as their ministers. The witnesses note the an offering to the LORD: given wholly to his work. The witnesses note the reconciled, then employed: the order of all true service. The expositors observe the after atonement: the Levites first cleansed and atoned. The expositors observe the before Aaron and his sons: presented as their ministers. The expositors observe the an offering to the LORD: given wholly to his work. The expositors observe the reconciled, then employed: the order of all true service. The believer is taught to mark the after atonement, the before Aaron and his sons, the an offering to the LORD, the reconciled, then employed, and to be cleansed and given to God. The verse thus records that the Levites, atoned for, were set before the priests and offered to the LORD — first reconciled, then employed, the order of all true service.
14:
"Thus shalt thou separate the Levites from among the children of Israel: and the Levites shall be mine." The witnesses note the end of the whole rite — the Levites separated from Israel and made the LORD's own. The witnesses note that Gill gathers the means of separation: the sprinkling of the water of purification, the sacrifices, the presenting before the LORD with the people's hands laid on, the waving, and the setting before Aaron. The witnesses note that by all these the Levites were marked off and given to God. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the separate the Levites — marked off from among Israel; the by all the rites — cleansing, sacrifice, presentation, waving; the the Levites shall be mine — god's peculiar possession; the the goal of the rite — the servants set apart wholly to the LORD.

The witnesses agree the verse states the purpose of all the foregoing ceremonies: cleansing, sacrifice, presentation, and waving together accomplished one thing — to separate the Levites from the common body of Israel and make them God's peculiar possession; "the Levites shall be mine" is the goal of the whole rite, the servants of the sanctuary set apart wholly to the LORD who claimed them.

So the witnesses make the verse the Levites made God's: The witnesses note the separate the Levites: marked off from among Israel. The witnesses note the by all the rites: cleansing, sacrifice, presentation, waving. The witnesses note the the Levites shall be mine: God's peculiar possession. The witnesses note the the goal of the rite: the servants set apart wholly to the LORD. The expositors observe the separate the Levites: marked off from among Israel. The expositors observe the by all the rites: cleansing, sacrifice, presentation, waving. The expositors observe the the Levites shall be mine: God's peculiar possession. The expositors observe the the goal of the rite: the servants set apart wholly to the LORD. The believer is taught to mark the separate the Levites, the by all the rites, the the Levites shall be mine, the the goal of the rite, and to be wholly given to God. The verse thus records that the whole rite separated the Levites and made them the LORD's — "the Levites shall be mine," the servants of the sanctuary set apart wholly to God.
15:
"And after that shall the Levites go in to do the service of the tabernacle of the congregation: and thou shalt cleanse them, and offer them for an offering." The witnesses note that only after the cleansing and offering might the Levites go in to their service. The witnesses note that Gill notes they went not into the sanctuary, where only the priests entered, nor the most holy place, but into the court of the tabernacle. The witnesses note that Ellicott reads them going into the court to keep watch, assist the priests at the altar, and take down and set up the tabernacle. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the after the cleansing — first purified and offered; the go in to the service — admitted to the work of the tabernacle; the in the court — not the holy places of the priests; the clean before serving — no holy work but by the purified.

The witnesses agree the order is again pressed — first cleansed and offered, then admitted to serve: the Levites' work lay in the court, not the holy places reserved for the priests, yet even that lower service was not entered until they were purified and dedicated; the lesson stands that no degree of holy service is to be undertaken but by those first made clean and given to God.

So the witnesses make the verse admitted to serve: The witnesses note the after the cleansing: first purified and offered. The witnesses note the go in to the service: admitted to the work of the tabernacle. The witnesses note the in the court: not the holy places of the priests. The witnesses note the clean before serving: no holy work but by the purified. The expositors observe the after the cleansing: first purified and offered. The expositors observe the go in to the service: admitted to the work of the tabernacle. The expositors observe the in the court: not the holy places of the priests. The expositors observe the clean before serving: no holy work but by the purified. The believer is taught to mark the after the cleansing, the go in to the service, the in the court, the clean before serving, and to be wholly given to God. The verse thus records that the Levites, once cleansed and offered, went in to serve in the court — no holy service entered but by those first made clean and given to God.
16:
"For they are wholly given unto me from among the children of Israel; instead of such as open every womb, even instead of the firstborn of all the children of Israel, have I taken them unto me." The witnesses note the ground of the Levites' separation — they are wholly given to God, taken instead of the firstborn. The witnesses note that Gill notes the emphatic "given, given," repeated either for confirmation or because they were twice given — first to the Lord by Israel, then by the Lord to Aaron. The witnesses note that they were taken in place of all the firstborn that open the womb. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the given, given — the emphatic word of full surrender; the twice given — to the Lord by Israel, then to Aaron; the instead of the firstborn — taken in their place; the substitution — one tribe given that the firstborn go free.

The witnesses agree the doubled word marks how completely the Levites belonged to God: wholly and certainly given, taken in place of the firstborn whom God claimed from the night of the Passover; the Levites stood as the redemption-price of the nation's firstborn, a tribe given to God that the firstborn might go free — substitution running through the whole arrangement, one given for many.

So the witnesses make the verse wholly given to God: The witnesses note the given, given: the emphatic word of full surrender. The witnesses note the twice given: to the Lord by Israel, then to Aaron. The witnesses note the instead of the firstborn: taken in their place. The witnesses note the substitution: one tribe given that the firstborn go free. The expositors observe the given, given: the emphatic word of full surrender. The expositors observe the twice given: to the Lord by Israel, then to Aaron. The expositors observe the instead of the firstborn: taken in their place. The expositors observe the substitution: one tribe given that the firstborn go free. The believer is taught to mark the given, given, the twice given, the instead of the firstborn, the substitution, and to be wholly given to God. The verse thus records that the Levites were wholly given to God in place of the firstborn — the redemption-price of the nation, substitution running through the whole arrangement.
17:
"For all the firstborn of the children of Israel are mine, both man and beast: on the day that I smote every firstborn in the land of Egypt I sanctified them for myself." The witnesses note God's claim on all the firstborn, hallowed to himself on the day he smote Egypt. The witnesses note that Gill notes the firstborn, both man and beast, are God's own. The witnesses note that Clarke refers to the manner of redeeming the firstborn (Num. 18:16). The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the all the firstborn are mine — man and beast, God's own; the the day I smote Egypt — spared and hallowed in the Passover; the sanctified for myself — claimed by right of redemption; the the redeemed God's — belonging to him who spared them.

The witnesses agree the Levites' calling reaches back to the redemption of the Exodus: on the night the destroyer passed through Egypt and spared Israel's firstborn, God hallowed them all to himself as his own, bought out of death; the Levites now stand in their place, the whole order resting on that night of blood and deliverance — a standing memorial that the redeemed belong to God by right of his sparing them.

So the witnesses make the verse the firstborn hallowed: The witnesses note the all the firstborn are mine: man and beast, God's own. The witnesses note the the day I smote Egypt: spared and hallowed in the Passover. The witnesses note the sanctified for myself: claimed by right of redemption. The witnesses note the the redeemed God's: belonging to him who spared them. The expositors observe the all the firstborn are mine: man and beast, God's own. The expositors observe the the day I smote Egypt: spared and hallowed in the Passover. The expositors observe the sanctified for myself: claimed by right of redemption. The expositors observe the the redeemed God's: belonging to him who spared them. The believer is taught to mark the all the firstborn are mine, the the day I smote Egypt, the sanctified for myself, the the redeemed God's, and to be wholly given to God. The verse thus records that the firstborn are God's, hallowed on the night he smote Egypt — the Levites standing in their place, the redeemed belonging to God by right of his sparing them.
18:
"And I have taken the Levites for all the firstborn of the children of Israel." The witnesses note God taking the Levites in exchange for all the firstborn of Israel. The witnesses note that Gill notes God gave up his right in the firstborn, and instead took the Levites for his use and service. The witnesses note that the exchange was God's own arrangement. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the taken the Levites — for all the firstborn of Israel; the God's right released — his claim on the firstborn given up; the a substitute accepted — levi serving in their stead; the the firstborn freed — the one given that the many go free.

The witnesses agree the verse states the substitution plainly: God released his claim on every firstborn of Israel and took the tribe of Levi in their stead, that the one might go free and the other serve; the exchange shows the grace of God, who, having a right to all the firstborn, was pleased to accept a substitute, foreshadowing the great exchange by which the one stands for the many and the bound are set at liberty.

So the witnesses make the verse the exchange made: The witnesses note the taken the Levites: for all the firstborn of Israel. The witnesses note the God's right released: his claim on the firstborn given up. The witnesses note the a substitute accepted: Levi serving in their stead. The witnesses note the the firstborn freed: the one given that the many go free. The expositors observe the taken the Levites: for all the firstborn of Israel. The expositors observe the God's right released: his claim on the firstborn given up. The expositors observe the a substitute accepted: Levi serving in their stead. The expositors observe the the firstborn freed: the one given that the many go free. The believer is taught to mark the taken the Levites, the God's right released, the a substitute accepted, the the firstborn freed, and to be wholly given to God. The verse thus records that God took the Levites for all the firstborn — releasing his claim and accepting a substitute, foreshadowing the exchange by which the one stands for the many.
19:
"And I have given the Levites as a gift to Aaron and to his sons from among the children of Israel... and to make an atonement for the children of Israel: that there be no plague among the children of Israel..." The witnesses note the Levites given as a gift to Aaron, that they bear the service and that no plague come on Israel. The witnesses note that Gill notes the LORD gave to Aaron and his sons the Levites that were first given to himself. The witnesses note that Ellicott reads the appointment of the Levites in the firstborn's place as a safeguard, "that there be no plague." The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the given to Aaron — the Levites a gift to the priests; the to bear the service — doing the work of the sanctuary; the no plague — keeping the people from holy danger; the the few guarding the many — the ministry a mercy to all.

The witnesses agree the Levites' service was a mercy to the whole nation: by bearing the holy things and serving the sanctuary, they kept the common Israelites from drawing near to their hurt, that no plague break out as when the holy is profaned; the ministry of the few protected the many, the Levites standing between the people and the danger of the holy, a guard set for the safety of all.

So the witnesses make the verse the gift and the guard: The witnesses note the given to Aaron: the Levites a gift to the priests. The witnesses note the to bear the service: doing the work of the sanctuary. The witnesses note the no plague: keeping the people from holy danger. The witnesses note the the few guarding the many: the ministry a mercy to all. The expositors observe the given to Aaron: the Levites a gift to the priests. The expositors observe the to bear the service: doing the work of the sanctuary. The expositors observe the no plague: keeping the people from holy danger. The expositors observe the the few guarding the many: the ministry a mercy to all. The believer is taught to mark the given to Aaron, the to bear the service, the no plague, the the few guarding the many, and to be wholly given to God. The verse thus records that the Levites were given to Aaron to serve and to keep plague from Israel — the ministry of the few a guard and mercy to the whole people.
20:
"And Moses, and Aaron, and all the congregation of the children of Israel, did to the Levites according unto all that the LORD commanded Moses concerning the Levites, so did they to them." The witnesses note the full obedience of Moses, Aaron, and all Israel in consecrating the Levites. The witnesses note that Gill names the several parties concerned in the consecration of the Levites. The witnesses note that Calvin notes the Levites inaugurated in their proper order, their condition inferior, the sons of Moses placed in this lower rank and excluded from the priesthood. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the Moses, Aaron, and all — the whole congregation obeying; the according to the command — done as the LORD ordered; the the inferior rank — even Moses' sons among the common Levites; the each his own place — the higher and lower of God's ordering.

The reformed reader (Calvin) marks a sobering point of history: even the descendants of Moses were ranked among the common Levites, shut out from the priesthood that went to Aaron's line, so that the later ingratitude of the tribe of Levi, and of the whole people, was the more inexcusable; God's arrangements are not to be envied or resisted, each appointed his own place, the higher and the lower alike of his ordering.

So the witnesses make the verse the ordered obedience: The witnesses note the Moses, Aaron, and all: the whole congregation obeying. The witnesses note the according to the command: done as the LORD ordered. The witnesses note the the inferior rank: even Moses' sons among the common Levites. The witnesses note the each his own place: the higher and lower of God's ordering. The expositors observe the Moses, Aaron, and all: the whole congregation obeying. The expositors observe the according to the command: done as the LORD ordered. The expositors observe the the inferior rank: even Moses' sons among the common Levites. The expositors observe the each his own place: the higher and lower of God's ordering. The believer is taught to mark the Moses, Aaron, and all, the according to the command, the the inferior rank, the each his own place, and to be wholly given to God. The verse thus records that all Israel consecrated the Levites in obedience — even Moses' line ranked among the common Levites, each appointed his own place by God's ordering, not to be envied or resisted.
21:
"And the Levites were purified, and they washed their clothes; and Aaron offered them as an offering before the LORD; and Aaron made an atonement for them to cleanse them." The witnesses note the Levites purified and washed, offered by Aaron, and atoned for to cleanse them. The witnesses note that Gill notes they were purified from sin by the sprinkling of the water of purification. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch gather the whole — purified, clothes washed, offered as an offering, and atonement made. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the purified — cleansed from sin by the water; the washed their clothes — the outward cleansing done; the offered by Aaron — presented before the LORD; the atonement made — by water and blood, made fit to serve.

The witnesses agree the verse records the carrying-out of all that was commanded: the Levites were cleansed within and without, presented to God, and atoned for, and so made fit for their service; the doubling of "purify" and "atone" and "cleanse" shows that nothing less than a thorough purifying — by water and by blood — fitted them to draw near, the same double cleansing the believer needs to serve God acceptably.

So the witnesses make the verse the cleansing carried out: The witnesses note the purified: cleansed from sin by the water. The witnesses note the washed their clothes: the outward cleansing done. The witnesses note the offered by Aaron: presented before the LORD. The witnesses note the atonement made: by water and blood, made fit to serve. The expositors observe the purified: cleansed from sin by the water. The expositors observe the washed their clothes: the outward cleansing done. The expositors observe the offered by Aaron: presented before the LORD. The expositors observe the atonement made: by water and blood, made fit to serve. The believer is taught to mark the purified, the washed their clothes, the offered by Aaron, the atonement made, and to be wholly given to God. The verse thus records that the Levites were purified, offered, and atoned for — a thorough cleansing by water and blood, made fit to serve, the double cleansing the believer needs.
22:
"And after that went the Levites in to do their service in the tabernacle of the congregation before Aaron, and before his sons: as the LORD had commanded Moses concerning the Levites, so did they unto them." The witnesses note the Levites at last entering on their service, all done as the LORD commanded. The witnesses note that Gill notes they went into the court of the tabernacle, having been at its door when the rites of consecration were performed. The witnesses note that the whole was done exactly as the LORD had commanded Moses. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the went the Levites in — to their work in the tabernacle; the before Aaron and his sons — serving under the priests; the as the LORD commanded — done in exact obedience; the the ministry established — the worship of God fully manned.

The witnesses agree the chapter ends on the same note as the others, exact obedience: the Levites, fully cleansed and consecrated, entered upon their service under the priests, and all was done as commanded; the repeated "so did they" seals the orderly establishing of the Levite ministry, the servants of the sanctuary now set in their appointed work, the worship of God fully manned.

So the witnesses make the verse entering on service: The witnesses note the went the Levites in: to their work in the tabernacle. The witnesses note the before Aaron and his sons: serving under the priests. The witnesses note the as the LORD commanded: done in exact obedience. The witnesses note the the ministry established: the worship of God fully manned. The expositors observe the went the Levites in: to their work in the tabernacle. The expositors observe the before Aaron and his sons: serving under the priests. The expositors observe the as the LORD commanded: done in exact obedience. The expositors observe the the ministry established: the worship of God fully manned. The believer is taught to mark the went the Levites in, the before Aaron and his sons, the as the LORD commanded, the the ministry established, and to be wholly given to God. The verse thus records that the consecrated Levites entered on their service in obedience — the Levite ministry orderly established, the worship of God fully manned.
23:
"And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying," The witnesses note the fixing now of the time of the Levites' service — when they should begin and when leave off. The witnesses note that Gill notes that at the same time as the instructions for their consecration, the time of their entrance on service and of leaving it was fixed. The witnesses note that the term of service was now to be set. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the a new word — the LORD speaking again to Moses; the the time fixed — when to begin and when to leave off; the strength and age — the burden on the strong, the aged spared; the framed with mercy — each serving in his season.

The witnesses agree the chapter closes by ordering the span of the Levites' working life: God appointed both when they should enter and when they should cease the heavy service, that the burden fall on those of strength and the aged be spared; the very hours of a man's service were under God's ordering, the ministry framed with wisdom and mercy, each serving in his season and resting in his age.

So the witnesses make the verse the time of service: The witnesses note the a new word: the LORD speaking again to Moses. The witnesses note the the time fixed: when to begin and when to leave off. The witnesses note the strength and age: the burden on the strong, the aged spared. The witnesses note the framed with mercy: each serving in his season. The expositors observe the a new word: the LORD speaking again to Moses. The expositors observe the the time fixed: when to begin and when to leave off. The expositors observe the strength and age: the burden on the strong, the aged spared. The expositors observe the framed with mercy: each serving in his season. The believer is taught to mark the a new word, the the time fixed, the strength and age, the framed with mercy, and to be wholly given to God. The verse thus records that God now fixed the time of the Levites' service — the ministry framed with wisdom and mercy, each serving in his season and resting in his age.
24:
"This is it that belongeth unto the Levites: from twenty and five years old and upward they shall go in to wait upon the service of the tabernacle of the congregation:" The witnesses note the age of entering service — from twenty-five years old the Levite went in to wait upon the tabernacle. The witnesses note that Gill notes this is the fixed and settled time for their service. The witnesses note that Keil and Delitzsch set the rule — from twenty-five years old and upward they went in to wait upon the service. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the from twenty-five — the Levite going in to wait; the the settled time — the fixed rule of their service; the learning first — the lighter service before the heavy; the full burden at thirty — harmonising with the earlier muster (Num. 4:3).

The witnesses note an apparent difference from the earlier muster, where the Levites were numbered from thirty (Num. 4:3): the common harmonisation, which the voices touch elsewhere, is that the Levite entered at twenty-five to wait, learn, and do the lighter service, but took up the full and heavy burden at thirty — apprenticeship before full charge, the younger trained under the elder before bearing the weight of the work.

So the witnesses make the verse the age of entering: The witnesses note the from twenty-five: the Levite going in to wait. The witnesses note the the settled time: the fixed rule of their service. The witnesses note the learning first: the lighter service before the heavy. The witnesses note the full burden at thirty: harmonising with the earlier muster (Num. 4:3). The expositors observe the from twenty-five: the Levite going in to wait. The expositors observe the the settled time: the fixed rule of their service. The expositors observe the learning first: the lighter service before the heavy. The expositors observe the full burden at thirty: harmonising with the earlier muster (Num. 4:3). The believer is taught to mark the from twenty-five, the the settled time, the learning first, the full burden at thirty, and to be wholly given to God. The verse thus records that the Levite entered service at twenty-five — to wait and learn before the full burden at thirty, apprenticeship before full charge.
25:
"And from the age of fifty years they shall cease waiting upon the service thereof, and shall serve no more:" The witnesses note the age of release — at fifty the Levite ceased the heavy service. The witnesses note that Gill notes they ceased the heavy service of the tabernacle. The witnesses note that the burden was lifted from the aging Levite at fifty. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the from fifty years — the term of heavy service ended; the cease waiting — released from the laborious work; the strength past — the years of full vigour over; the an honourable discharge — the old spared the heavy burden.

The witnesses agree the law dealt mercifully with the aging servant: at fifty the Levite was released from the laborious work of carrying and setting up the tabernacle, his years of full strength being past; God did not drive his servants to the end of their powers but gave them an honourable discharge from the heavy burden, the ministry framed to the measure of a man's strength, sparing the old what their failing bodies could no longer bear.

So the witnesses make the verse the age of release: The witnesses note the from fifty years: the term of heavy service ended. The witnesses note the cease waiting: released from the laborious work. The witnesses note the strength past: the years of full vigour over. The witnesses note the an honourable discharge: the old spared the heavy burden. The expositors observe the from fifty years: the term of heavy service ended. The expositors observe the cease waiting: released from the laborious work. The expositors observe the strength past: the years of full vigour over. The expositors observe the an honourable discharge: the old spared the heavy burden. The believer is taught to mark the from fifty years, the cease waiting, the strength past, the an honourable discharge, and to be wholly given to God. The verse thus records that at fifty the Levite ceased the heavy service — God sparing the aging servant, the ministry framed to the measure of a man's strength.
26:
"But shall minister with their brethren in the tabernacle of the congregation, to keep the charge, and shall do no service. Thus shalt thou do unto the Levites touching their charge." The witnesses note that the aged Levite, though freed from heavy labour, still ministered with his brethren in lighter service. The witnesses note that Gill notes he ministered by giving advice, instructing younger Levites, and doing lighter service, keeping the charge of the tabernacle, that no stranger or unclean person enter, but doing no heavy service. The witnesses note that the older Levite kept watch and counselled, fit for this though past the laborious work. The witnesses note, walking the verse through: the minister with their brethren — still serving, though freed from the burden; the keep the charge — guarding the tabernacle and its holy things; the instruct the younger — the experience of years turned to counsel; the no heavy service — the aged given a fitting work, not cast aside.

The witnesses agree the old age of the Levite was not idle but honoured: released from the burden, he still served by keeping watch, guarding the holy things, and teaching the younger; the experience of years was turned to counsel and oversight, the aged minister not cast aside but given a fitting work — a pattern that there is service for every season of life, the old guiding what the young carry.

So the witnesses make the verse the elder's lighter service: The witnesses note the minister with their brethren: still serving, though freed from the burden. The witnesses note the keep the charge: guarding the tabernacle and its holy things. The witnesses note the instruct the younger: the experience of years turned to counsel. The witnesses note the no heavy service: the aged given a fitting work, not cast aside. The expositors observe the minister with their brethren: still serving, though freed from the burden. The expositors observe the keep the charge: guarding the tabernacle and its holy things. The expositors observe the instruct the younger: the experience of years turned to counsel. The expositors observe the no heavy service: the aged given a fitting work, not cast aside. The believer is taught to mark the minister with their brethren, the keep the charge, the instruct the younger, the no heavy service, and to be wholly given to God. The verse thus records that the aged Levite still ministered in lighter service, keeping watch and teaching the young — service for every season, the old guiding what the young carry.
